items.question_text: An LOA permits SUA transit with using agency coordination. The using agency clears two aircraft through the area at different altitudes. What separation responsibility does the controller retain?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-4a, the controller must provide applicable separation minima between aircraft when two or more are transiting the area. Even though the using agency authorizes the transit, separation between transiting aircraft remains the controller's responsibility.

choices.text: Only vertical separation is required; lateral separation is delegated to the using agency.
The using agency assumes all separation responsibility for aircraft inside the SUA.
Separation is not required because the using agency's clearance deconflicts the aircraft.
Provide applicable separation minima between the two aircraft transiting the area.

items.question_text: A TEAL mission aircraft at FL 310 releases a dropsonde while a nonparticipating aircraft is at FL 210, 8 NM ahead on the same track. Who determines whether to delay the dropsonde release, and on what basis?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-19, the aircraft commander bears responsibility to delay release of dropsondes based solely upon traffic as issued by ATC. The controller provides the traffic advisory; the commander decides whether to proceed or delay.

choices.text: The controller, who must cancel the release if any traffic is within 10 NM of the drop point.
The aircraft commander, based solely upon the traffic information issued by ATC.
The supervisory traffic management coordinator, who deconflicts the drop with flow control.
The NOAA mission coordinator on the ground, who relays the decision through HF radio.

items.question_text: An AWACS/NORAD Special flight has requested FL 350. During expedited handling, a trainee assigns FL 320 to resolve a crossing conflict. Is this assignment within acceptable limits?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-18a, en route altitude changes are acceptable within ±2,000 feet of the requested flight level. FL 320 is 3,000 feet below the requested FL 350, exceeding the 2,000-foot tolerance. The maximum descent would be to FL 330.

choices.text: Yes. Any altitude change that resolves a conflict is acceptable for expedited handling.
No. Acceptable altitude changes are limited to ±2,000 feet from the requested flight level.
Yes. The ±2,000-foot limit applies only to climbs, not descents.
No. Altitude changes from the requested flight level are never permitted.

items.question_text: A FLYNET aircraft is already in contact with Center when it enters your approach control airspace. The pilot notes that DEN notification was accomplished by Center. What must your facility do?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-5b, the first facility establishing contact reports to the OS/CIC for DEN notification. Once DEN notification has been accomplished (as noted in the flight plan remarks per 9-2-5c), subsequent facilities do not need to re-notify. However, each facility must still provide expeditious handling. The DEN notification attaches to the flight, not to each facility.

choices.text: Transfer the aircraft back to Center because approach control cannot handle FLYNET aircraft directly.
Re-accomplish DEN notification because every ATC facility that handles a FLYNET aircraft must independently notify the DEN.
Provide routine handling since DEN notification was already done by the first facility.
Provide expeditious handling but do not re-accomplish DEN notification since it is already noted in the flight plan remarks.

items.question_text: A SAMP aircraft requests an altitude change for its nuclear sampling mission. Other IFR traffic is already established at the requested altitude. What is the controller's obligation?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, controllers must approve inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes to the maximum extent possible, and other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that SAMP requests are approved. The standard requires active accommodation — not just processing the request, but potentially moving other traffic to make it work.

choices.text: Approve the change only if the SAMP aircraft accepts responsibility for separation from the other traffic.
Deny the request because the altitude is already occupied by other IFR traffic.
Approve the altitude change to the maximum extent possible; reclear the established traffic if necessary.
Defer the request until the other IFR aircraft has passed the SAMP aircraft's position.

items.question_text: How does the controller's obligation for interceptor aircraft on active air defense scramble missions differ from the obligation for flight inspection aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-7, interceptor assistance continues only until the unknown aircraft is identified — it has a defined termination condition. Flight inspection assistance (9-1-1) continues for the duration of the inspection mission and has no automatic termination upon identification. Additionally, interceptors require expedited transfer of control jurisdiction upon ADCF request, a requirement unique to air defense missions.

choices.text: Both require maximum assistance for the entire flight; there is no operational difference.
Interceptor assistance terminates when the unknown aircraft is identified; flight inspection assistance continues for the mission duration.
Flight inspection aircraft have priority over interceptors in all cases.
Interceptor assistance only applies when the aircraft is squawking emergency codes.

items.question_text: A law enforcement aircraft requests that ATC locate and track a suspect aircraft. The controller's sector is busy with moderate traffic. What limits apply to providing this assistance?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12a2, assistance to law enforcement aircraft must not compromise approved separation minima or place the aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft. Maximum assistance is required, but it stops at the point where separation standards or safety would be compromised.

choices.text: Law enforcement assistance takes priority over all separation standards when suspect aircraft are involved.
Assistance must not compromise approved separation minima or place aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain or other aircraft.
Assistance is limited to communicating on a separate frequency; the controller cannot provide tracking.
Controllers must decline all law enforcement assistance requests when sector traffic is moderate or greater.

items.question_text: A parachute jump aircraft requests authorization in Class D airspace. The controller can see the jump aircraft on radar and has two VFR aircraft 5 miles from the jump zone. What is the correct authorization standard?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-3a, Class D parachute jumping is authorized 'with respect to known or observed traffic' — a more permissive standard than the Class A/B/C requirement to separate nonparticipating aircraft from the jump airspace (9-7-2b). In Class D, the controller weighs known traffic conditions and authorizes accordingly, but is not required to provide positive separation.

choices.text: Authorize without restriction; no traffic advisories are needed for Class D operations.
Deny the jump because nonparticipating VFR aircraft are within 10 miles of the drop zone.
Apply the same separation standard as Class A/B/C — separate all nonparticipating aircraft from the jump airspace.
Authorize jumping with respect to known or observed traffic; the standard is more permissive than Class A/B/C separation requirements.

items.question_text: When may a controller request a fuel-dumping aircraft to fly a different route?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-2, except when the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons, the controller may request it to fly a different route. Emergency fuel dumps are exempt from route-change requests — the aircraft's emergency takes priority over routing preferences. The rule applies in both VFR and IFR conditions.

choices.text: Only when the aircraft is operating under VFR, not IFR.
Except when the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons.
At any time, regardless of whether the dump is for emergency reasons.
Only when the aircraft is dumping fuel below 10,000 feet MSL.

items.question_text: What separation procedure applies to Prohibited or Restricted Areas established for security or non-ordnance hazardous activities?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-3c, certain Prohibited/Restricted Areas established for security reasons or to contain hazardous activities that do not involve aircraft ordnance are exempt from the added 500-foot vertical buffer. For these areas, the controller keeps aircraft clear of the airspace without applying the vertical buffer. For all other Prohibited/Restricted Areas, the buffer applies.

choices.text: At least 500 feet above or below the airspace limit, with 1,000 feet above FL 290.
Keep aircraft clear of the airspace without the added 500-foot vertical buffer.
At least 2,000 feet above or below the airspace limit at all altitudes.
Same separation as Warning Areas — 3 miles lateral or 500 feet vertical.

items.question_text: When separating an IFR aircraft from an aircraft dumping fuel, why is the required vertical separation different above and below the dumping aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-4, separation above the dumping aircraft is 1,000 feet (or per 4-5-1 if greater), while separation below is 2,000 feet. The larger separation below accounts for fuel descending and dispersing, which creates a greater hazard area beneath the dumping aircraft.

choices.text: Above separation is greater because aircraft climbing into falling fuel face the highest risk.
Fuel descends and disperses below the aircraft, creating a greater hazard beneath than above.
The difference is arbitrary and has no operational rationale.
Below separation is greater because ATC radar cannot reliably measure altitude below an aircraft dumping fuel.

items.question_text: How are special use frequencies assigned to ARTCCs?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-15 Note, special use frequencies are assigned to ARTCCs so that adjacent ARTCCs will not have the same frequency. They are used within the ARTCC area from the established FL base of high altitude sectors and above, with each high altitude sector having shared-use capability.

choices.text: All ARTCCs share the same special use frequency nationwide.
Adjacent ARTCCs are assigned different frequencies to prevent interference.
Each ARTCC sector within the same center receives a unique special use frequency.
Special use frequencies rotate between ARTCCs on a monthly schedule.

items.question_text: What three requirements govern parachute operations in Class A, Class B, or Class C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-2, controllers must: (a) authorize parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity; (b) separate nonparticipating aircraft from the authorized jump airspace; and (c) impose any conditions and restrictions that would promote safety of the operation.

choices.text: Authorize anywhere in Class A/B/C, provide traffic advisories only, and file a NOTAM after the operation.
Authorize only within designated airspace, separate nonparticipating aircraft, and impose safety conditions as needed.
Coordinate with the using agency, separate only IFR aircraft, and broadcast advisories to all aircraft.
Authorize per pilot request, separate only VFR aircraft, and notify the supervisor after each jump series.

items.question_text: What is a controller's responsibility regarding unusual aircraft activity near special interest sites such as nuclear power plants or dams?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-8, controllers must immediately relay any reports or information regarding unusual aircraft activities near special interest sites to supervisory/CIC personnel. Controllers have no independent monitoring or observation responsibilities unless directed by supervisory/CIC personnel.

choices.text: Vector all aircraft at least 3 NM away from these sites at all times.
Monitor these sites continuously using available radar and visual observation.
Immediately relay any reports or information to supervisory/CIC personnel.
Contact the Department of Homeland Security before taking any action.

items.question_text: At what points during a parachute operation must pertinent information be coordinated with affected ATC facilities?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-1, coordinate pertinent information prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps which begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction. Coordination is with other affected ATC facilities/sectors.

choices.text: Only after the jump is complete and the aircraft has left the area.
Prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps.
Only when the jump involves more than 10 parachutists.
At the beginning of each shift regardless of jump scheduling.

items.instruction: Identify the operation to which Section 3 SUA/ATCAA/ALTRV separation procedures still apply.
items.question_text: For which operation must the controller continue applying Section 3 separation procedures?

choices.text: The pilot informs ATC that permission has been obtained from the using agency to operate in the airspace.
The using agency informs ATC they have given permission for the aircraft to operate in the airspace.
The restricted area, MOA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV has been released to the controlling agency.
The aircraft is IFR and in radar contact at least 20 NM from the SUA boundary.

items.explanation: The IFR aircraft in radar contact remains subject to the procedures because those facts do not create an exception. Exceptions include using-agency permission, release of the airspace to the controlling agency, an approved ALTRV when the airspace is not an ATCAA, or an applicable letter of agreement. Alert Areas and Controlled Firing Areas are excluded from these procedures.

items.instruction: Determine whether the statement about special air traffic rules is correct.
items.question_text: A trainee states: 'Every airspace area listed in 14 CFR Part 93 is a Special Flight Rules Area.' Is this correct?

choices.text: Correct. Every airspace area listed in 14 CFR Part 93 is, by definition, a Special Flight Rules Area.
Incorrect. Not all Part 93 areas are SFRAs, but all SFRAs follow Part 93 rules.
Correct. SFRAs and SATRs are identical categories with the same operational rules.
Incorrect. Special Flight Rules Areas are defined in 14 CFR Part 91, not in Part 93.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-9, Special Flight Rules Areas are areas where flight is subject to special air traffic rules in 14 CFR Part 93 unless otherwise authorized by ATC. Not all areas listed in Part 93 are SFRAs — the distinction is that SFRAs have specific flight rules, while other Part 93 areas may have different regulatory structures.

items.instruction: Construct the correct phraseology for this situation using the prescribed format.
items.situation: An aircraft squawking the assigned SFRA code has been radar-identified. You need to advise the aircraft to proceed on course as requested while remaining outside Class B, C, and D airspace.
items.question_text: Which transmission uses the correct prescribed phraseology?

choices.text: N123AB, RADAR CONTACT, PROCEED AS FILED BUT STAY CLEAR OF ALL CONTROLLED AIRSPACE.
N123AB, TRANSPONDER OBSERVED, PROCEED ON COURSE AS REQUESTED BUT REMAIN OUTSIDE OF CLASS B, C, AND/OR D AIRSPACE AS APPROPRIATE.
N123AB, TRANSPONDER OBSERVED, CLEARED TO PROCEED ON COURSE, REMAIN CLEAR OF CLASS B AIRSPACE ONLY.
N123AB, SQUAWK OBSERVED, PROCEED ON COURSE AND MAINTAIN VFR AT ALL TIMES IN THE SFRA.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a phraseology, the correct transmission is: '(ACID) TRANSPONDER OBSERVED, PROCEED ON COURSE AS REQUESTED BUT REMAIN OUTSIDE OF CLASS B, C, AND/OR D AIRSPACE AS APPROPRIATE.' The key elements are 'TRANSPONDER OBSERVED' (not 'radar contact') and the specific airspace exclusion language covering B, C, and/or D as appropriate.

items.instruction: Evaluate the pilot readback against the prescribed IR clearance phraseology.
items.clearance: CLEARED INTO IR 801. MAINTAIN IR 801 ALTITUDES.
items.situation: The controller issues: 'CLEARED INTO IR 801. MAINTAIN IR 801 ALTITUDES.'
items.question_text: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the clearance?

choices.text: Roger, entering IR 801 at this time, Callsign 123.
Cleared into IR 801, maintaining flight level two zero zero, Callsign 123.
Cleared direct IR 801, will maintain VFR, Callsign 123.
Cleared into IR 801, maintaining IR 801 altitudes, Callsign 123.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-6 phraseology, the clearance is 'CLEARED INTO IR (designator). MAINTAIN (altitude)' or 'MAINTAIN IR (designator) ALTITUDE(S).' The correct readback mirrors the clearance: 'Cleared into IR 801, maintaining IR 801 altitudes.' The readback must include the IR designator and altitude restriction as issued.

items.instruction: Determine whether the controller may request a different route in each situation.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations may the controller request the fuel-dumping aircraft to fly a different route?

choices.text: An aircraft dumping fuel because of a dual hydraulic failure; the controller prefers an offshore route.
An aircraft dumping fuel because of an engine fire; the dump route passes over a populated area.
An aircraft dumping fuel for any reason — emergency routing changes are always permitted.
An aircraft dumping fuel to reduce weight for an overweight landing; the dump route passes over a populated area.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-2, the controller may request a different route except when the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons. The overweight landing (first option) is a non-emergency operation, so a route change may be requested. The engine fire and dual hydraulic failure are emergency situations where the aircraft cannot be asked to deviate. The rule applies to both VFR and IFR conditions.

items.front: When transiting SUA under an LOA/LOP, what two controller obligations exist?
items.back: 1. Comply with the using agency's instructions or clearances. 2. Provide applicable separation minima between aircraft when two or more are transiting the area.

items.front: What is the controller's fallback when unable to comply with using agency instructions during a coordinated SUA transit?
items.back: Clear the aircraft in accordance with nonparticipant separation minima (9-3-2): at least 500 feet vertical (1,000 feet above FL 290), 3 miles radar, or 3 miles lateral from the SUA boundary.

items.front: What is the relationship between the controller and the aircraft commander during a dropsonde release?
items.back: The controller advises the mission aircraft of traffic near the drop area. The aircraft commander decides whether to delay the dropsonde release — based solely upon the traffic information ATC provided.

items.front: What are the two distinct controller duties when a pilot's route or landing involves a hazardous radiation area?
items.back: 1. Flight path: Advise the pilot and reroute if requested. 2. Landing airfield: Inform the pilot the airfield is within the area and request the pilot to advise his/her intentions.

items.front: When a pilot's intended landing airfield is within a hazardous radiation area, what does the controller request?
items.back: The controller requests the pilot to advise his or her intentions. The controller does not automatically reroute or close the airport — the pilot decides how to proceed.

items.front: What three actions must the first ATC facility take when establishing contact with a FLYNET aircraft?
items.back: 1. Provide expeditious handling. 2. Report to the OS/CIC for DEN notification to the NTSO ATSC. 3. Add to the flight plan remarks that DEN notification has been accomplished.

items.front: Which aircraft can receive radar vectoring to a jettison drop area without a written agreement?
items.back: USAF, Air National Guard, and Air Force Reserve aircraft. Civil operators and civil aircraft companies require a written agreement in effect for the location.

items.front: What is required before civil operators can receive vectoring service for jettisoning external stores?
items.back: A written agreement must be in effect for the location. The regional counsel must approve the agreement, which relieves the FAA of possible liability.

items.front: What level of accommodation must controllers provide to SAMP aircraft requesting altitude or route changes?
items.back: Approve inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes to the maximum extent possible. Other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that SAMP requests are approved.

items.front: What is the operational difference between 'dangerous cargo' and 'inert devices' in a military flight plan?
items.back: Dangerous cargo contains hazardous materials requiring special handling. Inert devices contain NO dangerous materials but closely resemble nuclear or explosive devices in appearance — they also receive special handling to protect the mission profile.

items.front: What is the key operational difference between Class D and Class A/B/C parachute jump authorization?
items.back: Class A/B/C (9-7-2): Separate nonparticipating aircraft FROM the airspace authorized for jumping. Class D (9-7-3): Authorize jumping WITH RESPECT TO known or observed traffic — a judgment-based standard that does not require positive separation.

items.front: What are the four separation minima for nonparticipating aircraft from active SUA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV?
items.back: 1. At least 500 feet above/below the airspace limit (above FL 290: 1,000 feet). 2. 3 miles radar. 3. 3 miles laterally. 4. In accordance with a letter of agreement or letter of procedure.

items.front: What must ATC confirm before advising that MARSA is terminated during aerial refueling?
items.back: ATC must receive the tanker's report that both aircraft are vertically positioned within the refueling airspace. Only then does ATC advise MARSA terminated. MARSA does NOT automatically end when the receiver reports fueling complete.

items.front: What are the controller's responsibilities regarding unusual aircraft activity near special interest sites?
items.back: Immediately relay any reports or information regarding unusual aircraft activities near sites such as nuclear power plants, dams, or refineries to supervisory/CIC personnel. Controllers have no responsibility to monitor or observe aircraft near these sites unless directed.
